Assessing Outcome in Cognitive Behavior Therapy
for Child Depression: An Illustrative Case Series
Dikla Eckshtain & Scott T. Gaynor

Assessing Outcome in Cognitive Behavior
Therapy for Child Depression: An
Illustrative Case Series
DIKLA ECKSHTAIN and SCOTT T. GAYNOR
Department of Psychology, Western Michigan University, Kalamazoo, Michigan, USA

Recent meta-analytic data suggest a need for ongoing evaluation of
treatments for youth depression. The present article calls attention to
a number of issues relevant to the empirical evaluation of if and
how cognitive behavior therapy for child depression works. A case
series of 6 children and a primary caregiver received treatment—
individual CBT for the child and behavioral parent training
involving the caregiver and caregiver–child dyad. The effects were
generally promising and illustrate how selection of inclusion
criteria, measures, measurement intervals, and informants can
alter conclusions. These areas warrant attention in studies of child
depression and are important not only in interpreting treatment
outcome data but also for conducting sound clinical practice.

KEYWORDS
child depression, cognitive-behavior therapy, multi-
informant, multi-method

A recent meta-analysis of 35 psychosocial treatment studies for youth depres-
sion revealed only modest effect sizes for therapy compared to no treatment
(ES ¼ .41) and active control conditions (ES ¼ .24; Weisz, McCarty, & Valeri,
2006). The majority of the treatments included in the meta-analysis could
be considered cognitive-behavioral (CBT) in nature. This is noteworthy
because CBT has generally been considered the psychosocial treatment of
choice for youth depression (see Chorpita et al., 2002), but the mean effects
reported by Weisz and colleagues were substantially lower than in prior

reviews (e.g., Lewinsohn & Clarke, 1999; Reinecke, Ryan, & DuBois, 1998).
These data illustrate the need for ongoing evaluation of treatments for youth
depression, which is especially true for preadolescents where there is a
paucity of outcome research. For instance, in their meta-analysis, Weisz
et al. identified only seven studies with pre-teens compared to 21 with ado-
lescents. In addition, while several large-scale randomized trials have been
conducted with depressed adolescents (e.g., Brent et al., 1997; Clarke,
Rohde, Lewinsohn, Hops, & Seeley, 1999; Rohde, Clarke, Mace, Jorgensen,
& Seeley, 2004; The Treatment for Adolescents with Depression Study Team
[TADS], 2004), nothing comparable has been done with children. A greater
focus on adolescents is sensible in that the rates of depression rise dramati-
cally in the teen years. However, earlier-onset depression is clinically signifi-
cant and might indicate a unique and possibly more severe course (Birmaher
et al., 1996; Kovacs, 1996); thus, warranting research focus in its own right.
In interpreting the modest clinical outcomes with depressed youth, a
number of issues warrant consideration. One important area is how depres-
sion is measured, which has implications for the assessment of treatment
outcome directly, but also indirectly in determining which children are eligible
to be included in a study. To illustrate, consider the findings of Asarnow, Scott,
and Mintz (2002) that 10 twice weekly sessions of CBT was more efficacious
than a wait list condition for 4th–6th graders (N ¼ 23). Asarnow and colleagues
(2002) used a Child Depression Inventory (CDI; Kovacs, 1992) score of greater
than or equal to 8 to determine inclusion. The minimum inclusion criterion, a
score of 8, may appear quite modest given Kovacs’ report of a mean of 9.5 for
adjustment disorder with depressed mood and 11.8 for those with major
depressive disorder. Indeed, extrapolating from the CDI, Asarnow and
colleagues estimated that only 48% of the sample could tentatively be
considered to have met DSM criteria for major depression.
However, it is plausible that even if the CDI scores were relatively low
a greater proportion of the sample may have met DSM criteria for major
depression than the CDI data would suggest. In a study showing a moderate
benefit for fluoxetine compared to placebo (ES ¼ .51; Emslie et al., 2002),
children were required to meet DSM criteria and show at least moderate
depressive symptoms according to the clinician-administered, semistructured
Children’s Depression Rating Scale-Revised (Poznanski & Mokros, 1996),
which was also the primary dependent variable. Despite average CDRS-R
scores that were in the mid-50s, well above the authors’ cutoff for inclusion
of 40, the ‘‘mean baseline CDI scores were below the cut point for depression’’
(Emslie et al., 2002, p. 1210). In other words, Emslie et al. found that a group of
children required to meet DSM criteria and display moderate symptoms
according to a clinician administered interview, failed to show elevations on
the CDI. Given that clinician interviews are often considered the ‘‘gold stan-
dard,’’ it may be that Asarnow and colleagues’ sample was more severe than
their CDI scores suggested. Alternatively, given the lack of corresponding

Assessing Outcome
95

self-report data, it might be argued that Emslie et al.’s sample should be
considered more modest in their symptoms than the interview suggested.
The interpretive problem, in part, is one of a lack of additional informa-
tion to help qualify conclusions. Asarnow et al. (2002) did not report the
pretreatment group means on the CDI and did not collect additional
interviewer-administered or parent-rated symptom measures. As such, the
CDI served as the sole measure for study inclusion and evaluation of depres-
sive symptom outcome, complicating the interpretation of results. The same
argument can be made with respect to Emslie and colleagues’ (2002) failure
to present the self-report data in their study. In a previous study by that
group (i.e., Emslie et al., 1997), significant differences were observed on
interviewer measures, but no differences between fluoxetine and placebo
were found on parent or child self-report measures—a result which has been
found in most studies using Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs)
to treat child depression. As such, the efficacy data for SSRIs come almost
exclusively from clinician-administered interviews (Jureidini et al., 2004). If
children and parents do not see the problem or the effects of an intervention
in the same way or to the same degree that a clinician does, this is important
information.
One of the largest outcome studies with elementary-age children
compared eight sessions of Primary and Secondary Control Enhancement
Training (PASCET, a CBT intervention) to no treatment (Weisz, Thurber,
Sweeney, Proffitt, & LeGagnoux, 1997). The 48 3rd–6th grade participants
met inclusion criteria of either a CDI 11 or a CDRS-R (child informant) 34.
CBT resulted in significantly greater reductions in depressive symptoms on
the CDI (a decrease from 18.6 to 7.1 in CBT compared to 17.8 to 11.8 among
controls, ES ¼ .45). In addition, a greater amount of change was observed on
the CDRS-R for the CBT group (i.e., a decrease from 45.3 to 33.2) compared
to controls (a decrease from 38.4 to 34.9). The conclusions from this study
were consistent across self-report and interview measures. Data from other
informants were not taken.
The potential for disagreement across measurement methods leads to
the basic recommendation to take and report multiple measures using differ-
ent methods (e.g., self-report and clinical interview). Weisz et al. (2006)
found that on average two depression measures were collected in youth
depression studies; but, these were averaged in their analyses to produce a
single effect size for each study. Disagreement across measures, while possi-
ble, is only part of the interpretive dilemma. Another is disagreement across
informants. A large-scale meta-analytic review revealed correlations between
parent, teacher, child, and clinician reports on dimensional measures of child
(internalizing and externalizing) behavior that averaged about r ¼ .25
(Achenbach, McConaughy, & Howell, 1987). A review focusing on semistruc-
tured and structured diagnostic interviews also documented poor concor-
dance across informants, particularly parents and children (Grills &

96
D. Eckshtain and S. T. Gaynor

Ollendick, 2002). For instance, in a large study using the highly structured
NIMH Diagnostic Interview Schedule for Children (DISC; Shaffer et al.,
1996), it was found that parent-child agreement for depressive disorders pro-
duced Kappa coefficients near zero. Parent-adolescent agreement was a bit
better, but still poor, j  .20 (Jensen et al., 1999). Follow-up interviews by
clinicians of cases identified only by the parent or only by the child resulted
in ‘‘confirmation’’ of the depressive disorder diagnosis in only 41% and 30%
of the cases, respectively, revealing substantial clinician–parent and clinician–
child disagreement. With respect to parent–child disagreement, a further
complicating possibility comes from data suggesting a modest correlation
between maternal internalizing symptoms and their reports of their children’s
symptoms, which could potentially contribute to the discrepancy (Briggs-
Gowan, Carter, & Schwab-Stone, 1996).
The lack of concordance among measures and informants is relevant
to and can complicate interpretation of clinical outcome data. DeCuyper,
Timbremont, Braet, De Backer, and Wullaert (2004) compared 16 weekly
60-minute sessions of CBT to no treatment for children 9–13 with symptoms
of depression. The CDI decreased 2.6 points (from its baseline of 12.7) for the
CBT group (n ¼ 9) and 3.5 points (from its baseline of 15.3) for the waitlist
controls (n ¼ 11), suggesting no acute treatment effect for CBT according
to child self-report. However, according to parent-report on the Child Beha-
vior Checklist-Internalizing scale (CBCL-I), there was a change from baseline
(64.0) of 6.2 points for the CBT group, while in the control group the change
from baseline (67.5) was less than a point. The posttreatment CBCL-I effect
size of 0.99 (using Hedges correction for small samples) suggests a large
effect favoring CBT. (When the effect size is adjusted by subtracting the
pretreatment effect size from the posttreatment effect size, the result was a
medium effect size of .55 favoring CBT.) As such, conclusions about the
acute efficacy of CBT differed by informant. The Weisz et al. (2006) meta-
analysis data further support this contention. That is, their compilation of
studies produced only 6=35 that included both parent and youth reports of
depressive symptoms. Despite the small number of studies available for
analysis a significant difference was apparent, with larger effects for youth-
reported compared to parent-reported symptom change. Thus, despite the
DeCuyper et al. (2004) findings, the treatment efficacy data come mainly
from the reports of youth.
In the preceding pages it has been suggested that interpreting the results
of child depression studies is complicated by the use of single measures,
disagreement between measures when more than one is used, and disagree-
ment between informants completing measures on the same construct
(i.e., depressive symptoms). One additional complication is the lack of data
related to the time course of change in child depression studies. In CBT
for adult depression a great deal of change appears to occur very early in
treatment and may be due to nonspecific factors (Ilardi & Craighead,

Assessing Outcome
97

1994). With depressed adolescents, time course data suggest that across
treatment approaches about 25% experience notable improvements in
depressive symptoms prior to the start of treatment and over 25% experience
a large ‘‘sudden gain’’ early in the course of treatment (Gaynor et al., 2003).
Determining the trajectory and stability of symptoms in the baseline period
preceding treatment and the pattern of response during treatment can alter
how global pretreatment to posttreatment changes are interpreted. The time
course of change with preadolescents has received almost no attention in the
clinical outcome literature.
The present study illustrates the importance of multi-measure, multi-
informant repeated measures assessment. Six school-age children were
provided individual CBT (via the PASCET manual) plus Caregiver-Child
Relationship Enhancement Training (C-CRET; behavioral parent training
skills added by the authors to augment the skills taught in the PASCET man-
ual). Because of the potentially significant discrepancies between measures
and informants, a multi-measure, multi-informant approach to evaluating
change in depressive symptoms was used. Specifically, dimensional ques-
tionnaire data and categorical interview data from both the child and his=her
primary caregiver were obtained and used in the evaluation of outcome. In
addition, the short form of the CDI was completed at each session providing
information on the time course of change in depressive symptoms from the
perspective of the child.
METHOD
Participants

Six elementary school children, 5 females and 1 male (M ¼ 10; SD ¼ .75),
recruited=referred from Kalamazoo Center for Medical Studies=Michigan
State University, Department of Pediatrics participated (see Table 1). They
were required to report at least mild-to-moderate symptoms of depression
as assessed by a CDI of 8 (as in Asarnow et al., 2002) and a report of at least
an intermediate major depressive episode, by either parent or child report,
on the DISC (Shaffer, Fisher, Lucas, Dulcan, & Schwab-Stone, 2000). Out
of the 6 children, 5 lived with only one primary caregiver. Further, according
to the caregiver, 5 had a first degree relative with a history of depression,
who in four cases was the biological mother who also served as the study
informant. One child took medications but was eligible to participate
because he was on the same medication and dose for more than 2 months
prior to enrollment, met entry criteria, and his medication doses did not
change during the time of treatment. Two other children had received psy-
chotropic medicine previously, but were not currently medicated. Exclusion
criteria included primary symptoms of extreme Conduct Disorder, Obsessive-
Compulsive Disorder, schizophrenia-spectrum, autism-spectrum disorders,

or report of serious acute suicidality. Two children did not meet entry criteria
on the CDI and a third child met exclusion criteria due to Asperger’s
Disorder.
Design

All participants received PASCETþC-CRET. No concurrent waitlist group was
utilized. As such, this study was an uncontrolled open clinical trial. To
provide multiple assessments of outcome, diagnostic interviews, parent
checklists, and child self-report measures were administered at pre-
treatment, midtreatment, posttreatment, and 1-month follow-up. In addition,
child-reported depressive symptoms were measured at three baseline assess-
ments, prior to each treatment session, at 1-week posttreatment and
follow-up. These session-by-session data yield a series of single-case A=B
designs where A was baseline and B was PASCETþC-CRET.
Intervention

The individual treatment was based on the PASCET manual (Weisz, Moore, &
Southam-Gerow, 1999). The caregiver=caregiver-child sessions included the
psycho-educational meetings routinely used in PASCET plus 4 additional
sessions of C-CRET (Eckshtain & Gaynor, 2003) to augment the PASCET
skills. The treatment was broken into two phases separated by a mid-
treatment assessment. At the maximum, the intervention consisted of
13 individual meetings with the child, and 7 caregiver=caregiver-child
meetings (see Table 2). Each session lasted 75–90 minutes.
The PASCET program is a structured intervention for children ages 8–15.
The treatment is based on a two-process model of control. According to this
model, primary control involves enhancing reward or reducing punishment
by making, to the extent possible, objective conditions conform to the indi-
vidual child. Secondary control involves enhancing receipt of rewards or
reducing punishment by adjusting oneself to fit environmental conditions
so as to influence their subjective impact without altering the events them-
selves. Primary control is applied to distressing conditions that are modifiable
and secondary control to those conditions that are not (Weisz et al., 1999;
Weisz, Southam-Gerow, Gordis, & Connor-Smith, 2003).
PASCET caregiver sessions served the general functions of keeping the
caregivers informed, allowing for feedback regarding how the child is
doing outside the treatment setting, and encouraging the caregivers to assist
their children in generalizing the skills they worked on in the individual
sessions to the home environment. The added C-CRET sessions focused
on caregiver skills, including increasing the amount of positive time spent
together
between

and

positive
reinforcement,
noncontingent praise, enhancing communication and encouraging positive
communication,
familial

engagement in positive activities. Table 2 provides a summary of each
session and its major focus.
Treatment Integrity

All treatment sessions were conducted by the first author (DE), a doctoral
student in clinical psychology, who had completed graduate courses in child,
adult, and family behavior therapy. She had 2 years of clinical experience
working
with
children
conducting
both
assessments
and
cognitive-
behaviorally focused treatment. In addition, she received weekly supervision
from the second author (SG), a licensed clinical psychologist with research
and clinical experience using CBT with youth. To promote integrity, the
PASCET manual was studied in great detail prior to treatment implementation
and in developing the C-CRET sessions to accompany the PASCET manual.
In addition, prior to conducting each individual and caregiver meeting for
the first time, the therapist role-played the session with a research assistant.
To assess treatment adherence, after every session the therapist com-
pleted a Therapist Record Form. The PASCET forms were taken directly from
the PASCET manual developed by Weisz et al. (1999), while the C-CRET rat-
ing forms were developed for the current treatment using the same general

Assessing Outcome
101

format as used with the PASCET. As a check, all sessions were videotaped
and 37% of them were reviewed by graduate student coders in a doctoral
program in clinical psychology. The coders watched the tapes and
completed the Therapist Record Form. The coded sessions were chosen
randomly with the restriction that for each participant one session had to
come from the first four individual sessions, one from sessions 5–8, one from
sessions 9–13, and one caregiver session from the four C-CRET sessions. Com-
parison between the therapist and the three coders indicated 85% agreement,
11% disagreement, and 4% uncodable items, yielding j ¼ .72 (n ¼ 284, when
uncodable instances were coded as disagreement) and j ¼ .76 (n ¼ 274, when
uncodable instances were not included). The percent agreement and Kappa
coefficients suggested good to very good agreement between the therapist
and the coders (Cicchetti, 1994; Watkins & Pacheco, 2000). With 1.0 indicating
perfect adherence, the average therapist coding was .97 (SD ¼ .07) and the
average coders rating was .88 (SD ¼ .12), indicating that the therapist
implemented treatment with fidelity to the manuals.
Primary Measures

The Computerized NIMH DISC (Shaffer et al., 2000) was used to assess
psychopathology across the major diagnostic categories of the DSM-IV.
The modules for major depression, dysthymia, bipolar disorder, generalized
anxiety, social anxiety, conduct disorder, and Attention-Deficit=Hyperactivity
Disorder (ADHD) were administered at pretreatment, midtreatment, post-
treatment, and follow-up. The DISC is considered a reliable tool with
moderate to good validity in assessing child psychopathology (Schwab-Stone
et al., 1996; Shaffer et al., 1996). The DISC was administered to the children
(DISC-c) and to the caregivers regarding their children (DISC-p). For this
study, the clinical cutoff on the DISC was meeting DSM criteria for a major
depressive episode of at least intermediate severity.
The CDI (Kovacs, 1992) is a commonly used self-report measure of
depression for children that consists of 27 items assessing the presence
and severity of symptoms during the previous 2 weeks. The CDI has accep-
table internal consistency and test-retest reliability (Finch, Saylor, Edwards, &
McIntosh, 1987; Kovacs, 1992). The CDI was administered at pretreatment,
midtreatment, posttreatment, and follow-up. The 10-item CDI-Short Form
was administered to the children at the remaining baseline sessions and prior
to each treatment session. Kovacs (1992) reported a mean of 9.5 for adjust-
ment disorder with depressed mood and 11.8 for those with major depressive
disorder. The latter was considered the clinical cutoff in this study.
The Child Behavior Checklist-Parent Rating Form (CBCL; Achenbach &
Rescorla, 2001) is composed of 118 items assessing internalizing (CBCL-I)
and externalizing (CBCL-E) behaviors of the child. The CBCL has acceptable
test-retest reliability and content, construct, and criterion-related validity

102
D. Eckshtain and S. T. Gaynor

(Achenbach & Rescorla). Consistent with standard use of the CBCL, T scores
below 60 were considered in the normative range, scores from 60–63 in the
borderline range, and higher scores the clinical range.
Secondary Measures

The Cognitive Triad Inventory for Children (CTI-C; Kaslow, Stark, Printz,
Livingston, & Ling-Tsai, 1992) is a self-report measure for children consisting
of 36 items assessing their view of the self, world, and future. The CTI has demon-
strated acceptable internal consistency and concurrent validity, with depressed
children (in grades 4–7) averaging 39.5 (11.3) and control children 63.3 (5.1).
The
Children’s
Negative
Cognitive
Error
Questionnaire
(CNCEQ;
Leitenberg, Yost, & Carroll-Wilson, 1986) is a 24-item self-report measure
assessing cognitive errors (i.e., overgeneralization, catastrophizing, personaliz-
ing, and selective abstraction). The CNCEQ is reported to have test-retest reli-
ability of .65 for the total score. Average scores of 70.3 for depressed children
(in grades 4, 6, and 8) and 42.1 for nondepressed children were reported.
The Matson Evaluation of Social Skills for Youngsters (MESSY; Matson,
Rotatori, & Helsel, 1983) is a self-report measure of social skills, which has
demonstrated acceptable internal consistency and test-retest and split-half
reliability (Matson, Esveldt-Dawson, & Kazdin, 1983; Matson, 1994). The
items making up the Appropriate Social Skills, Inappropriate Assertiveness,
and Impulsivity scales were completed by the child (MESSY-c). The later
two were subtracted from the former, such that higher scores suggested bet-
ter social skills. Normative averages for 9- to 12-year-olds according to this
scoring method are 46 for girls and 37 for boys (Matson, 1994). Caregivers
were given the teacher-report version of the MESSY (MESSY-p). This version
consists of 64 items and has been used with caregivers in other research
yielding a normative mean (SD) of 139 (25; Bell-Dolan & Allan, 1998).
The Self-Report Measure of Family Functioning (SRMFF; Bloom, 1985) is
a 75-item measure completed by the primary caregiver. The SRMFF has been
reported to have adequate psychometric properties. In addition, young adults
who reported coming from disrupted families described their families differ-
ently (M ¼ 184) from young adults who reported coming from non-disrupted
families (M ¼ 196; Bloom). A family mental health history was obtained as part
of a demographic questionnaire. The caregiver informant responded to the
question ‘‘Does anyone in the child’s immediate or extended family have
the following illnesses or problems?’’ by indicating the relevant family mem-
ber next to each of the applicable 24 possible problem areas listed.

Note. CTI: 39.50 (11.30) for depressed children, 63.3 (5.1) for control children (grades 4 to 7; Kaslow et al., 1992).
CNCEQ: 70.3 for depressed children, 42.1 for nondepressed children (grades 4, 6, and 8; Leitenberg et al., 1986).
MESSY-c: 65.2 is normative data for girls, 78.3 for boys (Spence & Liddle, 1990).
MESSY-p: 138.6 (25.1) is normative data from parents (Bell-Dolan & Allan, 1998).
CBCL: T score of 64 or greater is considered in the clinical range (Achenbach & Rescorla 2001).
SRMFF: 184 (clinical), 196 (control), based on retrospective report of young adults from disrupted (clinical) and nondisrupted (control) families (Bloom, 1985).

As is apparent in Table 3, P1 exceeded clinical cutoffs on all 4 measures
at pretreatment. At midtreatment she no longer met criteria for a major
depressive episode on the DISC-p, but did continue to exceed clinical cutoffs
on the other 3 measures. However, by posttreatment she only exceeded cut-
offs on the CBCL-I. These results were maintained at 1-month follow-up (i.e.,
CDI ¼ 6, no DISC depression, CBCL-I ¼ 74). The time series data (Figure 1)
suggest relatively stable symptoms across baseline, which, like the DISC-c,
did not reliably decrease until the second half of the treatment where a
steady decline was noted with the lowest point at the posttreatment assess-
ment. On the secondary measures, the only potential elevation was on the
SRMFF, which was midway between the clinical and control sample averages
at pretreatment (see Table 4). However, family functioning improved by mid-
treatment and remained improved at posttreatment. Thus, P1 demonstrated
depressive symptom change on both the interview and self-report measures
for which she was the respondent and on 1=2 measures for which her
primary caregiver was the informant. The primary caretaker identified
the changes in depression and family functioning as occurring within the
first half of the treatment, while the child noted the most change during
the second half of the treatment.
P2 exceeded clinical cutoffs on 3 measures at pretreatment, with the
exception being the DISC-c. She showed a dramatic decrease on the CDI
by midtreatment, which was maintained at posttreatment, decreasing the
number of clinical cutoffs met from 3 to 2. The CDI time course data showed
a modest dip in symptoms at the final baseline point, which rebounded to the
previous level prior to session 1. However, by the start of session 3 she
reported a dramatic decline in depressive symptoms, which was maintained
through the remainder of treatment. Thus, according to her self-report P2
showed significant change early in the course of treatment that was main-
tained. That said, no depression change occurred according to maternal
report. The same pattern was observed on the secondary measures. That
is, according to maternal report P2 was also demonstrating significant exter-
nalizing behavior and social skills problems at all assessments. P2 did not
report problematic social skills, but did report a depressotypic cognitive style
at pretreatment, which was substantially improved by midtreatment (CTI &
CNCEQ) and showed additional improvement at posttreatment (CNCEQ).
Thus, according to child self-report substantial changes in depressive
symptoms and depressotypic thinking occurred early in the course of treat-
ment, while no changes were noted by maternal report on any dimensional
measures or the diagnostic interview. P2 did not participate in the follow-up
assessment.
P3 began treatment above the clinical cutoff on both parent-rated
measures, but neither child self-report measure. By midtreatment P3 no
longer exceeded clinical cutoffs on the DISC-p or CBCL-I. Maintenance
or further improvement was observed at posttreatment and at 1-month

Assessing Outcome
107

follow-up where she also failed to meet any clinical cutoffs: no DISC-c or
DISC-p depression diagnosis, CDI ¼ 0, and CBCL-I ¼ 54. The time course data
suggest initially mild depressive symptoms that dropped precipitously at the
second baseline session (which, unbeknownst to the therapist when the ses-
sion was scheduled, happened to be her birthday) and returned to mild levels
during the last two baseline sessions. These mild symptoms then remained
stable across the initial two thirds of treatment, with a decline in the final five
sessions to a low level. On the secondary measures P3’s mother reported a
small decrement in externalizing (from the borderline to nonclincial range)
from pretreatment to midtreatment, and a substantial improvement in family
functioning during the second half of treatment. In sum, both P3 and her
mother identified a change in depressive symptoms. P3’s mother identified
the most change in the first half of treatment with ongoing improvement in
the second half. P3’s self-reported change occurred primarily in the second
half, the time frame in which her mother reported improvements in family
functioning.
P4 terminated treatment after 7 individual sessions and 6 parent sessions
due to family difficulties, including living far away, transportation problems,
her mother’s emotional and physical health, her father’s health, and some
disagreement between the parents on the need for ongoing treatment. As
such, data are only available for P4 through midtreatment. At pretreatment
P4 met 3=4 clinical cutoffs. The exception was the DISC-c. At midtreatment
she showed decreased symptoms sufficient to move from meeting 3 cutoffs
to 1. Despite a CBCL-I score of 67, which continued to exceed the cutoff, a
notable decrease was observed from the baseline score of 82. A similar
decrement was noted on the CBCL-E as was an improvement in parent-rated
social skills. P4’s repeated measures data suggest that she experienced sub-
stantial change in symptoms during the baseline period before the onset of
treatment. Thus, the self-reported decrease in depressive symptoms does
not appear attributable to the treatment.
P5 met pretreatment clinical cutoffs on only 1 measure, the DISC-p. By
midtreatment P5 no longer met criteria for a depressive episode on the
DISC-p, a result which was maintained at posttreatment. The time course
data (see Figure 1) suggest that P5 reported mild depressive symptoms dur-
ing baseline that were modestly worsening at the last baseline measurement
and during the initial portion of treatment. However, session-by-session data
suggest that about 6–8 sessions into treatment P5 experienced a relatively
abrupt change in depressive symptoms to a low level that was maintained
throughout treatment. Thus, the DISC-p and CDI data suggest depression
change was concentrated during the latter portion of the first half of treat-
ment. An improvement in family functioning was also reported during this
time period. However, dimensional change on the CBCL-I was only observed
from midtreatment to posttreatment. No clinical cutoffs were exceeded at the
1-month follow-up, CDI ¼ 1, CBCL-I ¼ 48.

108
D. Eckshtain and S. T. Gaynor

At baseline P6 met clinical cutoffs on both caregiver-rated measures. A
slight worsening was observed at midtreatment where he also exceeded the
cutoff on the CDI in addition to the CBCL-I and DISC-p. However, significant
improvement occurred by posttreatment, as P6 no longer exceeded clinical
cutoffs on any depression measure (but did still meet DISC-p criteria for
ODD). At 1-month follow-up these gains were maintained: no DISC depres-
sive disorder diagnosis, CDI ¼ 5, and CBCL-I ¼ 53. The time course CDI data
suggest only a small change during the course of treatment. Two modest
‘‘spikes’’ in symptoms were observed where his CDI increased. At the begin-
ning of the second half of treatment his CDI scores decreased to their lowest
level, which was outside the baseline range. This decrease was maintained
for eight assessments before slightly increasing at posttreatment and then
decreasing by follow-up. Thus, for both P6 and his caregiver, the changes
in depression were most apparent in the second half of treatment. Interest-
ingly, at midtreatment, and therefore before any documented symptom
change in depression, P6 reported an improvement in his thoughts about
himself, the world, and future and a modest decrement in cognitive errors.
Improvement on the former remained stable during the second half of
treatment, while the latter showed marked additional improvement.
DISCUSSION

Measurement issues may complicate the evaluation of treatment studies for
child depression. Reliance on single measures, disagreement between mea-
sures (when more than one was used), disagreement between informants
(competing measures on the same construct), and a lack of time course data
may impact who is included in research studies and the data bearing on if
and how they work. In the present study, enrollment required meeting an
inclusion threshold according to either child or caregiver report on either a
categorical or dimensional measure. If a single measure had been used to
determine inclusion the number of participants would vary from 1=6 to
6=6 depending on the measure. Based on clinical cutoffs, only a single
participant exceeded both categorical and dimensional measures across
informants, with two others exceeding cutoffs on one child measure and
the caregiver measures. Across informants, the greatest pretreatment dis-
agreement was on meeting the diagnostic threshold on the DISC. All six care-
givers endorsed sufficient DISC symptoms, but only one of the children
concurred. Dimensionally, 3=6 children exceeded the CDI cutoff, while
5=6 were in the clinical range on the CBCL-I, again suggesting greater inter-
nalizing symptom endorsement by the caregivers. For P1–P4 the informant
was her biological mother who, in each case, reported a significant personal
history of mental health problems that included depression (see Table 1). As
such, it is noteworthy that these informants produced the highest CBCL-I

Assessing Outcome
109

scores. However, 3=4 of these children (P1, P2, & P3) had the highest initial
self-reported CDI scores as well. Thus, the dimensional data did not suggest a
dramatic mother-child discrepancy associated with the informant’s mental
health history. Instead, these pretreatment data are more consistent with
the suggestion that a significant caregiver mental health history may be
related to elevated child internalizing symptoms (Briggs-Gowan et al.,
1996). Within informants, caregivers demonstrated good pretreatment relia-
bility across the DISC-p and CBCL-I as 5=6 (83%) reports exceeded cutoffs
on both. Children’s agreement across measures was also generally good—
three were not elevated on either the dimensional or categorical measure
and one was elevated on both, suggesting agreement in 4=6 (67%) cases.
Depending on the measure and informant, the number of treatment
responders ranged from 40–100%. At the conclusion of their treatment, 3=6
failed to meet any clinical cutoffs. Those who met more self- and informant-
rated clinical cutoffs at pretreatment (P1, P2, & P4) met fewer at their final
assessment, but did continue to meet at least one clinical cutoff at their
final assessment. With respect to specific measures, on the CDI 6=6 children
reported reductions in symptoms over the course of the treatment. All three
who exceeded clinical cutoffs at pretreatment no longer did so at either mid-
treatment or posttreatment. On the DISC-p, 5=6 children went from meeting
criteria for a major depressive episode to no longer meeting criteria during
the treatment period. On the CBCL-I, 4=6 showed dimensional decreases
in internalizing problems, with 2=5 (who were initially above the criterion)
no longer meeting the clinical threshold at posttreatment. For the three
who still met the CBCL-I cutoff at his=her final assessment, the data were
provided by a maternal caregiver with a history of mental health problems
that included depression. These data suggest a potential role for this variable
in accounting for the outcome discrepancy on the dimensional measure.
However, it is important to note that two of these mothers reported a
decrease on the DISC-p resulting in no depressive disorder diagnosis and that
our measure of maternal mental health was an affirmative response on a
family history item list. No measure of the informant’s current symptoms
was collected. In sum, these data are consistent with findings in the adoles-
cent literature that pretreatment severity is related to reduced likelihood of
meeting remission cutoffs (Clarke et al., 1992; Curry et al., 2006; Gaynor &
Lawrence, 2002) and that the findings were generally strongest on youth-
report measures (Weisz et al., 2006).Given the addition of the C-CRET ses-
sions, it is also interesting to note that the three families with the lowest
self-reported
family
functioning
at
pretreatment
reported
substantial
improvements in this area over the course of treatment.
The current results are not from a randomized clinical trial and do not
employ the strongest possible single-case strategies (e.g., multiple baseline
design); thus, the generally positive outcome results must be viewed with
these design limitations in mind. The purpose of this article; however, is

110
D. Eckshtain and S. T. Gaynor

not to offer strong conclusions regarding efficacy but to highlight some
important interpretive and measurement issues and to point out their
implications for future research (single-participant and group designs) and
clinical practice.
One ready recommendation is to use and report data from multiple
measures on key constructs. This allows for documentation of method
variance that can be used to clarify or qualify sample characteristics and
outcome conclusions. Such a recommendation may seem obvious given
the fairly longstanding recognition of the issue (see Kazdin, 1989) but, as
reviewed in the Introduction, numerous recent studies of child depression
treatment have failed to implement this practice. It is also worth remember-
ing that what is being measured are multi-factorial constructs. Depression is a
summary label applied when a group of five or more signs and symptoms
are present most of the day, everyday, for a couple of weeks (American
Psychiatric Association, 2000). This definition of depression allows for
substantial subjectivity and variability in reporting (e.g., in deciding whether
to respond in the affirmative to whether sad mood has been present ‘‘most’’
of the day and nearly everyday). Thus, it is not that one informant is neces-
sarily correct (e.g., the child is depressed), while another is incorrect (e.g, the
child is not depressed). Each brings his=her level of psychological distress,
perspective, experiences, biases, interpretations, and imperfect recall to the
reporting situation (De Los Reyes & Kazdin, 2005). Furthermore, in some
cases certain behaviors of the child may be more salient and more likely to
guide responding for one informant than others and, in some cases, control-
ling variables other than absolute accuracy may enter into the reporting
(e.g., attempts to deny problems or to locate the source of problems with
a particular individual).
Identification of discrepancies (or lack thereof) is not only important for
evaluating research, but also for conducting treatment in clinical practice
settings. Recent data suggest that in clinical settings parents and children
often disagree on even the broad problem(s) for which services are sought
(Yah & Weisz, 2001). Such disagreement can present quite a challenge for
therapists in establishing agreed upon treatment goals (Hawley & Weisz,
2003) and may be a marker of parent-child conflict more generally (De Los
Reyes & Kazdin, 2006). As such, taking measures from multiple informants
is not just of informational value (to determine if inclusion=exclusion criteria
are met, to get as well-rounded a view of the child=family as possible, etc.)
but may point to the first treatment goal—to establish agreement on goals.
The pervasive lack of agreement on target problems may be one reason that
children fail to complete a full course of treatment (Nock & Ferriter, 2005)
and may also reduce the effectiveness of treatment (Hawley & Weisz).
A number of variables differentiate research therapy from clinic-based
care (Weisz, Donenberg, Han, & Weiss, 1995) and the outcomes achieved
in clinical trials are not always replicated when treatment is applied in

Assessing Outcome
111

clinical practice settings. Part of the problem may be due to a lack of
understanding of how treatment for depressed youth works when it does
(Weersing & Weisz, 2002). Taking repeated measures of relevant process
(e.g., depressotypic thoughts, family relations) and outcome variables
(e.g., depressive symptoms) can be important in better understanding
the change process. For instance, the repeated measures data for 4=6 par-
ticipants (P2, P4, P5, P6) suggest that the majority of their self-reported
improvement in depressive symptoms happened over a very brief (1–2
week) interval. The timing of the change was different between the parti-
cipants—P4’s occurred prior to treatment, P2’s early in treatment, and P5’s
and P6’s in the middle of treatment—but the fact that the observed change
was concentrated over a brief interval (and maintained thereafter) was
similar. These data suggest that, for some, the change process may be
abrupt rather than gradual. For the remaining two participants the majority
of change on the repeated measure was concentrated in the later portion
of treatment and was more gradual. That the sample was comprised of
those demonstrating pretreatment gains, sudden within-treatment gains,
and gradual gains are consistent with findings with depressed teens
(Gaynor et al., 2003). What these patterns mean is unclear (especially
since the caretaker data did not always agree with the pattern identified
in the child’s repeated measures), but a better understanding of them
appears important in determining why change occurs.
More research is needed to determine the efficacy of CBT in the
treatment of children with depressive symptoms. To most usefully interpret
the findings and their clinical implications, multiple measures of key con-
structs, from the perspective of numerous informants, taken at repeated time
intervals throughout treatment should be collected.
